AI-Assisted Coding for Economists

Trainer: Eduard Bukin

2026-04-20

## Course Overview

Generative AI (Gen AI) and foundational large language models (LLM)
offer extraordinary opportunities to improve our analytical workflow and
daily life. By their design, LLMs are probabilistic: they predict the
most probable combination of characters, words, and sentences that could
follow a **prompt** like a human had written the prompt and what follows
it. Probabilistic nature implies that a single prompt could yield an
infinite number of completions. Therefore, the user’s objective is to
make AI’s responses as deterministic as possible. Precision and accuracy
of the AI’s answers determine its usefulness. Therefore, to harness full
power of the AI-Agents, we need to acquire new skills of:

·       Writing effective prompts;

·       Curating model context, and

·       Use the right **interfaces/software** for different tasks with
AI.

The **objective of this course** is to enable economists who perform
micro-data analysis with Stata/R (Python) to **revolutionize their
workflow** by leveraging context-aware AI assistance and agents. To
achieve this objective, this course will consist of short presentations
and, mostly, practical demonstrations, exercises, and self-study
materials, where we will:

·       Introduce WB-approved software and solutions:

o   Integrated Development Environment (IDE): Positron

o   Github.com/Worldbank:

§  Collaboration platform and

§  WB Open-Source initiative for analysis development

o   Github Copilot:

§  WB-approved way of using AI assistance (Anthropic Claude) for coding.

·       Help troubleshoot their installation and use.

·       Discuss key principles and shortcomings of AI assistance in
daily coding work.

o   Overview of technology, its strengths, and limitations.

o   Responsible use of AI: securing sensitive data and limiting the
context.

o   Effective prompting.

·       Practice key skills of the context-aware use of AI:

o   In-line code completion: instant boost to your work

o   AI Assistance: almost a replacement to google with the context-aware
answer

o   AI Agents: delegate complex tasks to AI to perform independently on
your behalf.

o   Planning coding with AI.

o   Advanced topics: Custom AI-Agents, Instructions and Skills.

·       Practice AI use on typical use-cases:

o   Code refactoring, cleaning and commenting.

o   Reproducibility package preparation.

o   Reproducing old code using new data.

The course is structured in two 3-hours sessions of (preferably)
in-person and/or hybrid work at the beginning and the end of the week,
and a period of self-study between two sessions with the structured
exercises.

-

- **The 1st session** covers key concepts, troubleshoot software setup
  and usage, and provides a kick-off demonstration of AI capabilities
  for coding and ways of using it. By the end of this first session,
  participants should be capable of using AI on their computers
  independently and be enabled to learn it further.

-

- **Self-Study period** is meant to provide space and time for
  participants to practice AI use in analysis and get comfortable with
  the software interface and coding workflow. Using clearly defined case
  studies and examples, course participants are expected to resolve
  problems independently and accumulate questions for the second
  session, where these problems will be addressed.

-

- **The 2nd session** is dedicated to practicing specific skills and
  use-cases as well as addressing questions. We will demonstrate use of
  **AI for planning** and **executing complex tasks**; practice this
  with the participants, answer their questions, and conclude with the
  demonstration of the more advanced: cutting-edge use cases such as
  **agentic code development**, **prompt files creation**, **skills,**
  testing, and debugging.

-

To consider the course completed, participants are expected to
demonstrate the ability to use AI assistance for coding independently
and submit **solutions to case-studies** on the use of AI assistance for
coding during the self-study period, and in a week after the second
session. Successful submission is a requirement, which will be used as
deliverables for assessing the course's completion.

## Detailed plan

### Prerequisites

-

- Basic familiarity with Stata/R (Python) and micro-data analysis.

-

- Installation of Positron IDE and GitHub Copilot (instructions will be
  provided before the course).

-

o   Engaging with the ITS helpdesk for any software installation issues.

-

- Setting up a GitHub account, joining the World Bank organization and
  requesting access to the GitHub Copilot for World Bank (instructions
  will be provided before the course).

-

o   Engaging with [github\@worldbank.org](mailto:github@worldbank.org)
for any issues related to GitHub account setup, access to GitHub
Copilot.

### Session 1: Introduction to AI-Assisted Coding

-

- Live demonstration of a code refactoring task with and without AI
  assistance to show the difference in workflow and output quality.

-

- Walkthrough of the software interface and usage workflow:
  troubleshooting common issues.

-

- Overview presentation:

-

o   AI-assistance technology;

o   strengths and limitations: when DO and DO NOT use/expect AI to be
helpful,

o   responsible use of AI;

o   basics of prompting: how to write effective prompts;

o   curating context for AI: how to limit the context;

o   secure sensitive data.

o   Set expectations right: Stata support vs other languages - R and
Python.

-

- Case studies:

-

o   Refactoring code with AI assistance.

o   Writing with AI: preparing a reproducibility package and documenting
code.

-

- Q&A and discussion.

-

### Self-Study Period: Practice and Exploration

-

- During a 2–3-day self-study period, participants will be encouraged to
  perform structured exercises to practice AI-assisted coding, document
  their experiences, challenges, and questions during this period to
  bring to the second session.

-

- The Live Teams channel will be available for participants to ask
  questions and share insights during the self-study period.

-

- Participants will be asked to submit a case study completed with the
  help of the AI assistance during the self-study period, which will be
  used as one of the deliverables for assessing the course completion.

-

            Selected case studies for self-study:

-

  -

  - Reproducing old code using new data with AI assistance.

  -

  - Reproducing analysis following an example with AI assistance.

  -

  - Trying a different language: R or Python instead of Stata with AI
    assistance.

  -

### Session 2: Everyday AI-Assisted Coding and Use Cases

-

- Addressing questions and challenges from the self-study period.

-

- Demonstration of planning and executing complex tasks with AI
  assistance.

-

- Overview presentation

-

o   prompt engineering for complex tasks

o   prompt patterns as reusable templates.

o   when to stop using AI and think: recognizing when AI is leading you
in circles

o   cost of AI use and token awareness

o   enhancing the workflow with GitHub and Git-versioning: how to start
using the help from AI.

o   Getting right context: prompt files, skills, and custom agents:
where to start

-

- Practical case studies:

-

o   Planning and executing complex tasks with AI assistance.

o   Advanced use cases: agentic code development, testing, and
debugging.

o   Positron Data Bot: agent for fast exploratory data analysis.

-

- Q&A and discussion.

-
